agent_output:
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Memory leak from never-cleaned-up WebSocket listeners and CourierTracker instances, compounded by unhandled JSON parse errors that can crash the process."
  },
  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "Memory leak: WebSocket listeners and CourierTracker instances never cleaned up",
      "risk": "With couriers reconnecting every ~5 minutes, each connection creates a new CourierTracker with event listeners bound to the WebSocket. There is no 'close' listener to remove handlers, no reference cleanup, and no teardown method. The CourierTracker instance, its closure over `this`, and the WebSocket listeners accumulate indefinitely. This is the direct cause of the reported 3-month memory growth.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "CourierTracker.setupListeners()",
      "reproduction": "1. Create 100 CourierTracker instances. 2. Close all 100 WebSockets. 3. Create 100 new ones. 4. Observe heap snapshot — old CourierTracker instances and their listener closures are retained because no 'close' event removes them and nothing dereferences the tracker.",
      "fix": "Add a 'close' listener in setupListeners that calls a teardown method. The teardown must: (1) remove 'message' and 'error' listeners via named function references and ws.removeListener(), (2) null out `this.ws` to break the reference cycle. Callers must also delete the CourierTracker from whatever map holds it. Example:\n\nprivate setupListeners(): void {\n  this.onMessage = (data: string) => { ... };\n  this.onError = (err: Error) => { ... };\n  this.ws.on('message', this.onMessage);\n  this.ws.on('error', this.onError);\n  this.ws.on('close', () => this.teardown());\n}\n\nprivate teardown(): void {\n  this.ws.removeListener('message', this.onMessage);\n  this.ws.removeListener('error', this.onError);\n  this.ws = null;\n}",
      "time_to_fix": "30 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Service stability — memory usage must remain bounded under sustained courier reconnection load"
    },
    {
      "title": "Unhandled JSON.parse throws on malformed WebSocket messages, crashing the process",
      "risk": "Any non-JSON message (binary frame, empty string, partial message from a disconnect) will throw a SyntaxError from JSON.parse. Since this runs inside a WebSocket 'message' event with no try/catch, the unhandled exception propagates. Depending on the Node.js error handling setup, this either crashes the process or triggers an unhandledRejection that goes silent. A single malformed packet from any courier kills the entire service.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "CourierTracker.setupListeners() — ws.on('message') callback",
      "reproduction": "Send a non-JSON string (e.g., empty string or 'ping') over the WebSocket. JSON.parse throws SyntaxError. If no global uncaughtException handler exists, the Node process exits.",
      "fix": "Wrap the message handler in try/catch:\n\nthis.ws.on('message', (data: string) => {\n  try {\n    const location = JSON.parse(data);\n    if (typeof location.lat !== 'number' || typeof location.lng !== 'number') return;\n    this.updateLocation(location);\n  } catch (err) {\n    console.warn(`Invalid message from courier ${this.courierId}:`, err.message);\n  }\n});",
      "time_to_fix": "10 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Service availability — a single bad message must not crash the process"
    },
    {
      "title": "Fire-and-forget database write with no error handling silently loses location data",
      "risk": "db.couriers.updateLocation is called with no await, no .catch(), and no callback. If the DB is slow, down, or rejects the write (e.g., invalid coordinates, connection pool exhaustion from the high reconnection rate), the failure is completely silent. Location data is lost with no indication, making courier tracking unreliable without any observable error.",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "CourierTracker.updateLocation()",
      "reproduction": "Bring the database down or exhaust the connection pool. Send location updates. No error is logged, no metric is emitted. Location data silently disappears. Under high reconnect churn, connection pool exhaustion is especially likely.",
      "fix": "Await or catch the DB call:\n\nprivate async updateLocation(location: { lat: number; lng: number }): Promise<void> {\n  try {\n    await db.couriers.updateLocation(this.courierId, location.lat, location.lng);\n  } catch (err) {\n    console.error(`Failed to update location for ${this.courierId}:`, err);\n    // emit metric for alerting\n  }\n}",
      "time_to_fix": "15 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Data integrity — location updates must be reliably persisted or failures must be observable"
    }
  ],
  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "No input validation on lat/lng coordinates",
      "description": "latitude and longitude are not validated for range (-90 to 90, -180 to 180) or type. A malicious or buggy courier client can write arbitrary values (NaN, Infinity, null) into the database, corrupting location data used for ETA calculations and dispatch logic.",
      "location": "CourierTracker.updateLocation()"
    }
  ],
  "suggestions": [
    "Consider rate-limiting location updates per courier (e.g., max 1/second) to prevent DB write storms from misbehaving clients",
    "Add a connection registry (Map<string, CourierTracker>) with cleanup on close to make the lifecycle explicit and observable",
    "Add a health metric tracking active CourierTracker count to detect future leaks early"
  ],
  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "The memory leak is already in production and worsening. The fix requires restarting the service, which will disconnect all active couriers simultaneously — coordinate with reconnection backoff.",
    "rollback_capability": "Rollback is safe since the fix only adds cleanup behavior; removing it returns to current (leaking) behavior.",
    "backward_compatibility": "No breaking changes — fixes are internal to the class.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "No monitoring exists. Add: active tracker count, DB write error rate, WebSocket parse error rate, heap size.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": false,
      "data_pipeline": false,
      "config_changes": false,
      "details": []
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "Revert to previous version; memory leak resumes but service remains functional until next restart."
  },
  "escalations": [],
  "confidence": {
    "level": 95,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Memory leak from missing WebSocket close/cleanup — this is the textbook cause of the reported symptom",
      "JSON.parse crash — deterministic failure on any non-JSON input",
      "Silent DB write failures — observable from the code with no catch/await"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Whether there is a global uncaughtException handler that prevents full process crash from JSON.parse — assumed none based on code shown"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "db.couriers.updateLocation returns a Promise",
      "No external lifecycle manager cleans up CourierTracker instances on disconnect",
      "No global uncaughtException handler catches the JSON.parse SyntaxError"
    ]
  }
}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this code for critical issues.

Key observations:
1. Memory leak: No cleanup of WebSocket listeners or CourierTracker instances when connections close
2. No JSON.parse error handling - malformed messages crash the handler
3. No input validation on lat/lng
4. Fire-and-forget DB call with no error handling

The memory leak is the critical finding given the stated symptom of steadily increasing memory usage over 3 months.

    {
      "title": "Memory leak: WebSocket listeners and CourierTracker instances never cleaned up",

There is no 'close' listener to remove handlers, no reference cleanup, and no teardown method. The CourierTracker instance, its closure over `this`, and the WebSocket listeners accumulate indefinitely. This is the direct cause of the reported 3-month memory growth.",

      "fix": "Add a 'close' listener in setupListeners that calls a teardown method. The teardown must: (1) remove 'message' and 'error' listeners via named function references and ws.removeListener(), (2) null out `this.ws` to break the reference cycle. Callers must also delete the CourierTracker from whatever map holds it. Example:\n\nprivate setupListeners(): void {\n  this.onMessage = (data: string) => { ... };\n  this.onError = (err: Error) => { ... };\n  this.ws.on('message', this.onMessage);\n  this.ws.on('error', this.onError);\n  this.ws.on('close', () => this.teardown());\n}\n\nprivate teardown(): void {\n  this.ws.removeListener('message', this.onMessage);\n  this.ws.removeListener('error', this.onError);\n  this.ws = null;\n}",
      "time_to_fix": "30 minutes",

    },

Depending on the Node.js error handling setup, this either crashes the process or triggers an unhandledRejection that goes silent. A single malformed packet from any courier kills the entire service.",

If the DB is slow, down, or rejects the write (e.g., invalid coordinates, connection pool exhaustion from the high reconnection rate), the failure is completely silent. Location data is lost with no indication, making courier tracking unreliable without any observable error.",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "CourierTracker.updateLocation()",
      "reproduction": "Bring the database down or exhaust the connection pool. Send location updates. No error is logged, no metric is emitted. Location data silently disappears. Under high reconnect churn, connection pool exhaustion is especially likely.",

      "time_to_fix": "15 minutes",

    }
  ],
